questions.question: Explain why population is considered the pivotal element in social studies.
questions.answer: Population is considered pivotal because people create, use and manage resources. All social and economic activities depend on human population.

questions.question: Explain the uneven distribution of population in India with reasons.
questions.answer: Population distribution in India is uneven due to differences in climate, terrain, soil fertility, water availability and economic opportunities.

questions.question: Explain the importance of census in population studies.
questions.answer: Census provides detailed demographic information including population size, distribution, literacy and occupation which help in planning development programmes.

questions.question: Explain the concept and importance of population density.
questions.answer: Population density refers to the number of persons living per square kilometre. It helps understand how crowded a region is and assists in planning infrastructure and services.

questions.question: Explain the factors influencing population density.
questions.answer: Population density is influenced by climate, terrain, soil fertility, availability of water and economic development.

questions.question: Explain the concept of population growth and its measurement.
questions.answer: Population growth refers to the increase in population over time and is measured through absolute increase and annual growth rate.

questions.question: Explain why India continues to add a large number of people each decade.
questions.answer: India adds many people because even a small growth rate applied to a large population base results in a large increase.

questions.question: Explain the processes of population change in detail.
questions.answer: Population change occurs through birth rate, death rate and migration which influence population size and distribution.

questions.question: Explain the role of birth rate in population growth.
questions.answer: Birth rate directly influences population growth. High birth rates increase the population rapidly.

questions.question: Explain the role of death rate in population growth.
questions.answer: Declining death rate leads to rapid population growth because fewer people die each year.

questions.question: Explain migration and its types.
questions.answer: Migration is the movement of people from one place to another. It can be internal or international migration.

questions.question: Explain the push factors of migration.
questions.answer: Push factors include poverty, unemployment, natural disasters and lack of facilities in rural areas.

questions.question: Explain the pull factors of migration.
questions.answer: Pull factors include employment opportunities, better education, healthcare and improved living conditions in cities.

questions.question: Explain the impact of rural to urban migration.
questions.answer: Rural to urban migration increases urban population and changes the demographic composition of both rural and urban areas.

questions.question: Explain the concept of urban population growth.
questions.answer: Urban population growth refers to the increase in people living in towns and cities due to migration and natural population increase.

questions.question: Explain the concept of million-plus cities.
questions.answer: Million-plus cities are cities with more than one million people, reflecting rapid urbanization.

questions.question: Explain the concept of adolescent population.
questions.answer: Adolescent population includes people aged 10–19 years and forms an important segment of the population.

questions.question: Explain the importance of adolescent population for development.
questions.answer: Adolescents form the future workforce and contribute to the country's economic and social development.

questions.question: Explain health challenges faced by adolescents in India.
questions.answer: Adolescents face problems such as poor nutrition, anaemia and lack of health awareness.

questions.question: Explain the role of education in population control.
questions.answer: Education increases awareness about family planning, health and responsible parenthood.

questions.question: Explain the objectives of National Population Policy 2000.
questions.answer: The policy aims to reduce infant mortality, promote education and ensure access to healthcare and family planning.

questions.question: Explain the importance of family welfare programmes.
questions.answer: Family welfare programmes help improve health conditions and control population growth.

questions.question: Explain why population planning is essential for sustainable development.
questions.answer: Population planning ensures balanced use of resources and prevents environmental degradation.

questions.question: Explain how population growth affects the environment.
questions.answer: Rapid population growth increases pressure on land, water and other natural resources.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between population growth and economic development.
questions.answer: Population growth influences economic development by increasing workforce but also putting pressure on resources.
